subtopic_detail: Non-accidental injury mandatory reporting and safety admission
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 6-month-old boy brought to the emergency department by his mother because he has stopped using his right leg. There is bruising on the medial thigh and the radiograph shows a transverse mid-shaft femoral fracture. His mother says he rolled off a low couch this morning, but a triage note states she earlier told the nurse he was kicked accidentally by his 3-year-old brother. The infant is not yet rolling on developmental assessment and weighs on the 25th centile. The maternal partner has been demanding to take the family home before assessment is complete and is becoming increasingly agitated.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.0, HR 144, RR 36, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Distressed with passive movement of right leg, swelling and bruising of the right medial thigh, 1 cm bruise to the left flank, no other external injuries identified, anterior fontanelle soft, no scleral haemorrhage
data_table.Investigations: Plain right femur radiograph: transverse mid-shaft femur fracture; FBC, UEC, LFTs, coagulation, calcium and CK pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Apply a Thomas splint, prescribe simple analgesia, refer to orthopaedics for outpatient closed reduction and follow-up, and discharge once the mother has signed an undertaking to attend the social work appointment next week with both parents
options.rationale: Outpatient discharge of an infant with a femur fracture and inconsistent history is unsafe; mandatory reporting and safety admission are required while assessment is completed. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Admit the infant under paediatrics for safety and analgesia, notify the statutory child protection service and senior paediatrician immediately, arrange a skeletal survey within 24 hours and request hospital security if there is any attempt to remove the child
options.rationale: A non-mobile infant with a femur fracture and a changing history meets criteria for mandatory child protection notification and inpatient safety admission with skeletal survey. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Request an urgent contrast computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis to look for hidden visceral injury and a head computed tomography to exclude intracranial haemorrhage, and defer child protection notification pending those imaging results
options.rationale: Child protection notification is not delayed pending imaging; it is mandatory on suspicion and runs in parallel with investigations. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Allow the family to leave the department with a follow-up paediatric appointment in 24 hours after a full ward discharge summary has been provided, after ensuring the orthopaedic plan is documented and analgesia has been prescribed for home use
options.rationale: Allowing discharge against safety concerns is unsafe and contravenes mandatory reporting obligations once child abuse is suspected. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Counsel the mother that this presentation is most likely accidental given the explanation of being kicked by a sibling, document the parents' account verbatim and discharge with primary health network referral for early childhood developmental support
options.rationale: A femur fracture in a non-mobile infant is a classic non-accidental injury pattern; accepting the carer's history at face value misses an at-risk presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

explanation.summary: A femur fracture in a non-mobile infant (under approximately 9 months) is highly concerning for non-accidental injury, particularly with a changing or implausible history. Mandatory reporting to the statutory child protection service is required on suspicion in every Australian jurisdiction, and notification is not delayed pending investigations. Safety admission allows skeletal survey within 24 hours, ophthalmology review for retinal haemorrhages if intracranial concerns, social work and paediatric forensic input. Imaging including head CT or MRI is added based on clinical features.
key_points: Femur fracture in a non-mobile infant is a non-accidental injury until proven otherwise
Mandatory child protection notification is on suspicion, not on confirmation
Safety admission allows skeletal survey, retinal exam if indicated, social work
explanation.pearls: Other concerning fracture patterns in young infants include posterior rib fractures, metaphyseal corner fractures, and any fracture in a non-mobile child without a clear major-force mechanism.

subtopic_detail: Septic arthritis hip urgent joint aspiration and IV antibiotics
stem: You are the paediatric emergency registrar assessing a 6-year-old boy with 36 hours of right hip pain, fever and refusal to weight-bear. He had a viral upper respiratory tract infection 10 days ago that has fully resolved. The hip is held in slight flexion, external rotation and abduction with marked guarding on attempted passive internal rotation. He has had no recent trauma, no skin breakdown and no rash. He has not had any episodes of haematuria or oliguria, and he is otherwise developmentally normal. Orthopaedics has been called for senior review and the operating theatre has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1, HR 130, RR 26, BP 102/64, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Right hip held in slight flexion external rotation, severe pain on attempted internal rotation, refuses to bear weight, no obvious effusion of the knee or ankle, no rash, mild right-sided inguinal tenderness, otherwise unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.6, neutrophils 14.2, CRP 92, ESR 56, blood cultures sent; hip ultrasound shows a significant right hip joint effusion compared to the left

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence oral ibuprofen and observe for 24 hours on the paediatric ward with rest and weight-bearing as tolerated, anticipating clinical resolution typical of transient synovitis of the hip following a recent viral illness
options.rationale: The Kocher criteria (fever above 38.5, non-weight-bearing, ESR over 40, WCC over 12) are all met giving over 99% probability of septic arthritis; oral analgesia alone is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Arrange a contrast magnetic resonance imaging of the hip before any intervention to differentiate transient synovitis, septic arthritis and osteomyelitis, and commence empirical oral cephalexin while awaiting the scan and ward review
options.rationale: MRI must not delay drainage in suspected septic arthritis; oral antibiotics are inadequate empiric cover for a hip joint with high clinical probability of infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Take blood cultures, transfer to operating theatre for urgent hip arthrotomy with washout and synovial fluid microscopy and culture, and commence intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly empirically immediately after the cultures
options.rationale: Septic arthritis of the hip is a surgical emergency requiring operative drainage and empirical IV flucloxacillin after cultures to cover Staphylococcus aureus, the dominant pathogen. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Commence intravenous vancomycin and ceftriaxone empirically to provide broad cover including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus and Kingella kingae, and admit under paediatric medicine for serial clinical review without aspiration
options.rationale: Antibiotics without joint drainage do not effectively clear septic arthritis; surgical washout is mandatory and routine MRSA cover is not required without local risk factors. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Trial intravenous ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg daily for 48 hours, reassess clinically and biochemically with repeat CRP and clinical examination at that point, and consider operative intervention only if no clinical or biochemical improvement
options.rationale: Delaying surgical washout of a septic hip risks femoral head necrosis; operative drainage is the time-critical intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic Arthritis)

explanation.summary: Septic arthritis of the hip in a child is a surgical emergency. The Kocher criteria (fever above 38.5, refusal to weight-bear, ESR over 40, white cell count over 12) predict over 99% probability when all four are met. Management is blood cultures, urgent operative arthrotomy or arthroscopic washout with synovial fluid sampling, and empirical IV flucloxacillin to cover Staphylococcus aureus. Ceftriaxone or vancomycin add-on is reserved for specific risk factors. Delaying drainage risks femoral head osteonecrosis and irreversible joint damage.
key_points: Kocher criteria 4/4 gives over 99% probability of septic arthritis
Surgical washout plus IV flucloxacillin is standard initial therapy
MRI must not delay drainage of a clinically septic hip
explanation.pearls: In children under 4 years Kingella kingae is an important pathogen; add cefazolin if Kingella suspected (low-grade fever, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, negative gram stain).

subtopic_detail: Sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis hydration and opioid analgesia
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing an 8-year-old boy with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) on regular folate and hydroxyurea, brought by his mother with severe right thigh pain since this morning. He has had a similar episode 6 months ago that resolved with admission. His baseline haemoglobin is 78 g/L and he is fully immunised including pneumococcal, meningococcal and Hib boosters. He has no current fever, no respiratory symptoms, no chest pain and no neurological symptoms. He has not been swimming or had any trauma. He weighs 24 kg and has been able to drink only sips of fluid since the pain commenced.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.1, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 98% room air, BP 108/68, pain score 8/10
data_table.Examination: Tender right thigh without erythema, swelling or restricted joint movement, chest clear, no focal neurology, no splenic enlargement
data_table.Investigations: Hb 76, retic 320, WCC 11.2, platelets 380, CRP 8, type and screen sent; chest X-ray clear; urine MCS pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Admit for intravenous fluids at 1.5 times maintenance with regular paracetamol, ibuprofen and intravenous morphine bolus then patient-controlled analgesia, supplemental oxygen if SpO2 drops, screen for acute chest syndrome with serial respiratory assessment and continue hydroxyurea
options.rationale: Vaso-occlusive crisis is managed with hydration at 1.5x maintenance, multimodal analgesia including parenteral opioid by PCA, and active monitoring for acute chest syndrome which is the leading cause of mortality. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)
source_refs: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines

options.text: Prescribe oral paracetamol and ibuprofen as required, encourage oral fluid intake and discharge home with general practice review in 24 hours and clear advice to return for any new fever, chest pain or breathing difficulty over the next week
options.rationale: Discharge without IV hydration and parenteral analgesia is inappropriate for severe sickle cell crisis with pain score 8 and poor oral intake; hospital admission is required. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)
source_refs: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines

options.text: Arrange urgent simple red cell transfusion of 10 mL/kg to a target haemoglobin of 100 g/L for all sickle cell vaso-occlusive crises on presentation, with prior cross-match using extended phenotyping for Rh and Kell antigens to minimise alloimmunisation
options.rationale: Transfusion is not routine for uncomplicated vaso-occlusive crisis; it is reserved for acute chest syndrome, stroke, splenic sequestration, aplastic crisis or symptomatic severe anaemia. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)
source_refs: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg daily empirically for occult bacteraemia given the underlying functional asplenia and severe pain, with discharge planned at 48 hours if cultures are negative and the patient is clinically improved
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are reserved for sickle cell disease patients with fever, signs of infection or acute chest syndrome; pain crisis alone without fever does not mandate antibiotics. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)
source_refs: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines

options.text: Arrange urgent exchange transfusion with leucodepleted packed red cells in the high-dependency unit to reduce HbS percentage below 30, with a peripheral large-bore intravenous access and continuous cardiac monitoring during the procedure
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for severe complications such as acute chest syndrome with hypoxia, stroke, or multi-organ failure; uncomplicated vaso-occlusive crisis does not warrant it. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)
source_refs: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines

explanation.summary: Paediatric sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis is managed with admission for IV hydration at 1.5 times maintenance, multimodal analgesia (paracetamol, NSAID if no contraindication, parenteral opioid by PCA if severe), warm packs, and active screening for acute chest syndrome (the leading cause of mortality) with serial respiratory observation, oximetry and chest examination. Hydroxyurea is continued. Transfusion is reserved for specific complications such as acute chest syndrome, stroke, splenic sequestration or aplastic crisis, and exchange transfusion for severe complications. Antibiotics are added if any features of infection or fever above 38.5.
key_points: IV fluids at 1.5x maintenance plus multimodal analgesia including parenteral opioid
Active screening for acute chest syndrome is essential
Transfusion is for ACS, stroke, sequestration or aplastic crisis, not routine VOC
explanation.pearls: Avoid pethidine due to seizure risk from norpethidine accumulation; use morphine or oxycodone for severe pain in paediatric sickle cell disease.

sources.label: HSANZ Paediatric Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Shwachman-Diamond syndrome recognition in failure to thrive with neutropenia and steatorrhoea
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 2-year-old boy referred from primary care for failure to thrive (weight tracking just below the 3rd centile, height on the 5th centile), recurrent respiratory and middle-ear infections over the past 12 months, and three months of bulky pale offensive stools that float and stain the nappy. He was born at term without complications. His parents are non-consanguineous of European ancestry. Sweat chloride testing performed twice at the regional centre has been normal at 22 and 18 mmol/L. He has no skin or hair changes, no organomegaly and no rash.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 110, RR 24, weight 10.4 kg, height 84 cm, BP 92/56
data_table.Examination: Mildly wasted appearance, mild abdominal distension, no organomegaly, no rash, normal cardiovascular and respiratory exam, normal external genitalia, no skeletal abnormalities on standard examination
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96, MCV 78, WCC 4.2 with neutrophils 0.4, platelets 240, faecal elastase 78 micrograms per gram (low), faecal fat increased, immunoglobulins normal, coeliac serology negative, IgA normal, two sweat chlorides normal (22 and 18)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation and management plan?

options.text: Repeat the sweat chloride test for a third time and proceed with empirical pancreatic enzyme replacement at 1000 lipase units per kilogram per meal and a high-energy formula, attributing the neutropenia to chronic inflammation while awaiting the repeat sweat result
options.rationale: Persistent neutropenia 0.4 with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and negative sweat chlorides is not cystic fibrosis; the syndrome to consider is Shwachman-Diamond. (Source: RCH CPG: Failure to Thrive)

options.text: Commence intravenous pancreatic enzyme replacement, dietitian-led calorie supplementation, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor 5 micrograms per kilogram subcutaneously daily, and refer immediately to oncology for empiric bone marrow transplant workup pending DNA result
options.rationale: Empirical GCSF and transplant workup precede confirmatory genetics; pancreatic enzymes are oral not IV; this plan misorders investigation and management. (Source: RCH CPG: Failure to Thrive)

options.text: Arrange a duodenal biopsy under general anaesthesia given the steatorrhoea and growth failure, send for histology to confirm villous architecture, and commence a gluten-free trial despite negative coeliac serology in case of IgA-deficient coeliac disease as a unifying diagnosis
options.rationale: Normal IgA and negative coeliac serology with profound neutropenia points away from coeliac disease; duodenal biopsy does not explain the bone marrow finding. (Source: RCH CPG: Failure to Thrive)

options.text: Request a skeletal survey, send SBDS gene testing for Shwachman-Diamond syndrome, refer to paediatric haematology, paediatric gastroenterology and a dietitian, commence oral pancreatic enzyme replacement and fat-soluble vitamins, and arrange neutropenia monitoring with infection precautions
options.rationale: The triad of exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, neutropenia and growth failure with normal sweat chlorides is Shwachman-Diamond syndrome; SBDS gene testing confirms diagnosis and skeletal survey screens for metaphyseal dysostosis. (Source: GHO Australian Paediatric Genetics)

options.text: Reassure that the constellation likely represents toddler diarrhoea and constitutional small stature, recommend daily faecal elastase repeat after a high-fat dietary trial, and review in 3 months in the general paediatrics clinic with weight and height plotted
options.rationale: Faecal elastase 78 with neutrophils 0.4 cannot be attributed to functional toddler diarrhoea; a unifying organic diagnosis must be pursued. (Source: RCH CPG: Failure to Thrive)

explanation.summary: Shwachman-Diamond syndrome is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by biallelic SBDS mutations characterised by exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, bone marrow dysfunction (most commonly neutropenia with risk of progression to aplastic anaemia and myelodysplastic syndrome/AML), and skeletal abnormalities (metaphyseal dysostosis). After two normal sweat chlorides this triad strongly points away from cystic fibrosis. Diagnosis is by SBDS gene testing. Management involves pancreatic enzyme replacement, fat-soluble vitamin supplementation, dietitian-led calorie support, regular FBE to monitor cytopenias, and bone marrow surveillance with haematology input.
key_points: Triad: pancreatic insufficiency + cytopenia (often neutropenia) + skeletal dysostosis
Sweat chloride normal rules out CF as cause of pancreatic insufficiency
SBDS gene testing confirms diagnosis; haem surveillance for MDS/AML risk
explanation.pearls: Approximately 20% of Shwachman-Diamond patients develop MDS or AML by adulthood, so lifelong haematology surveillance with annual bone marrow assessment is recommended in symptomatic disease.

subtopic_detail: Threatened miscarriage at 8 weeks expectant management
stem: You are the early pregnancy assessment service registrar reviewing a 30-year-old G2P1 woman at 8 weeks gestation by certain last menstrual period and a recent dating scan, who presents with 12 hours of light fresh vaginal spotting and mild lower abdominal cramping. She has had no passage of clots or tissue, no dizziness, no shoulder tip pain and her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated. She is rhesus D positive. Her observations are normal and abdominal examination is unremarkable. Speculum examination shows a small amount of fresh blood in the vault and a closed cervical os with no products of conception visible.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 84, BP 116/72, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Closed cervical os with light fresh blood in the vault, uterus consistent with dates, no adnexal tenderness, no peritonism on abdominal exam, bimanual examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: intrauterine gestational sac with single live foetal pole, crown-rump length consistent with 8 weeks 0 days, foetal heart rate 162 beats per minute, no subchorionic haematoma; beta-hCG 56000 IU/L; blood group O positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Commence intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg per square metre body surface area today to terminate the pregnancy because the bleeding represents an inevitable miscarriage which will be safer to complete medically than to await expectant resolution
options.rationale: Methotrexate is for tubal ectopic pregnancy; an intrauterine live pregnancy with threatened miscarriage is managed expectantly. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Arrange suction dilatation and curettage in theatre today to evacuate the uterine contents to reduce the risk of haemorrhage, infection and ongoing miscarriage symptoms, given the bleeding and the documented intrauterine gestational sac on ultrasound
options.rationale: Surgical evacuation is for incomplete or missed miscarriage; a viable live intrauterine pregnancy must not be terminated for threatened miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Admit overnight for observation, intravenous fluid maintenance, fasting from midnight and consent for emergency theatre in case of progression to incomplete miscarriage with heavy bleeding overnight, with morning gynaecology team reassessment
options.rationale: Inpatient observation is not warranted for a stable threatened miscarriage with light spotting and confirmed live intrauterine pregnancy; outpatient management is appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Prescribe vaginal micronised progesterone 400 mg twice daily as ovulation-induction support, advise prolonged bed rest at home with no work for two weeks, and arrange repeat early pregnancy ultrasound in three days for reassurance
options.rationale: Routine progesterone supplementation and bed rest are not effective for threatened miscarriage with no history of recurrent loss; PRISM trial showed benefit only with prior miscarriages. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Reassure that the live intrauterine pregnancy carries a high likelihood of progressing, provide written return precautions for heavy bleeding or severe pain, advise against intercourse for 7 days, and arrange repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 7 to 10 days for reassessment
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage with a confirmed live intrauterine pregnancy is managed expectantly with safety netting and a follow-up scan in 7-10 days; most progress beyond the bleeding episode. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

explanation.summary: Threatened miscarriage is defined as vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy with a closed cervix and a viable intrauterine pregnancy. Management is expectant with reassurance, safety netting and repeat ultrasound in 7-10 days, since most pregnancies continue beyond the bleeding episode. Anti-D immunoglobulin is offered only if rhesus negative and beyond 12 weeks gestation or for any intervention. Routine progesterone is not effective in women without prior miscarriages; the PRISM trial demonstrated modest benefit only in women with two or more prior losses.
key_points: Threatened miscarriage: bleeding with closed cervix and viable IUP - expectant management
Anti-D not routinely needed before 12 weeks unless intervention performed
Progesterone supplementation only if recurrent prior miscarriage (PRISM)
explanation.pearls: Subchorionic haematoma identified on ultrasound is associated with mildly increased miscarriage risk but does not change expectant management; activity restriction has no evidence base.
